perturbation.original_text: Emdeon may determine the placement and formatting of the Content in the Software, but may not change the substance of the Content to any extent whatsoever without WebMD's written approval of the change.
perturbation.changed_text: Emdeon may determine the placement and formatting of the Content in the Software, and will endeavor to avoid substantive changes to the Content without WebMD's consultation.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'may not change the substance...without WebMD's written approval' with 'endeavor to avoid substantive changes...without WebMD's consultation' weakens the obligation. The term 'endeavor to avoid' is subjective and doesn't guarantee adherence to the original content, nor does 'consultation' necessarily equate to written approval, creating ambiguity and allowing for potential unapproved changes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause protects WebMD's copyright by strictly prohibiting any substantive changes to the content without written approval. The modified clause, using 'endeavor to avoid', introduces ambiguity and potentially allows Emdeon to make unauthorized changes, infringing on WebMD's exclusive rights as the copyright holder under 17 U.S. Code § 106, which grants copyright owners the exclusive rights to reproduce, prepare derivative works, distribute copies, and display the copyrighted work publicly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: r, periodical, data base, electronic information service, electronic publication, or similar publication; (ii) any merchandising item or advertising, promotional, descriptive, covering, or packaging material or container; (iii) any portion or part of any item described in clause (i) or (ii); (B) any work made for hire; or (C) any work not subject to copyright protection under this title. 25 A “work of the United States Government” is a work prepared by an officer or employee of the United States Government as part of that person’s official duties. A “work made for hire” is— (1) a work prepared by an employee within the scope of his or her employment; or (2) a work specially ordered or commissioned for use as a contribution to a collective work, as a part of a motion picture or other audiovisual work, as a translation, as a supplementary work, as a compilation, as an instructional text, as a test, as answer material for a test, or as an atlas, if the parties expressly agree in a written instrument signed by them that the work shall be considered a work made for hire. For the purpose of the foregoing sentence, a “supplementary work” is a work prepared for publication as a secondary adjunct to a work by another author for the purpose of introducing, concluding, illustrating, explaining, revising, commenting upon, or assisting in the use of the other work, such as forewords, afterwords, pictorial illustrations, maps, charts, tables, editorial notes, musical arrangements, answer material for tests, bibliographies, appendixes, and indexes, and an “instructional text” is a literary, pictorial, or graphic work prepared for publication and with the purpose of use in systematic instructional activities. In determining whether any work is eligible to be considered a work made for hire under paragraph (2), neither the amendment contained in section 1011(d) of the Intellectual Property and Communications Omnibus Reform Act of 1999, as enacted by section 1000(a)(9) of Public Law 106-113, nor the deletion of the words added by that amendment— (A) shall be considered or otherwise given any legal significance, or (B) shall be interpreted to indicate congressional approval or disapproval of, or acquiescence in, any judicial determination, by the courts or the Copyright Office. Paragraph (2) shall be interpreted as if both section 2(a)(1) of the Work Made for Hire and Copyright Corrections Act of 2000 and section 1011(d) of the Intellectual Property and Communications Omnibus Reform Act of 1999, as enacted by section 1000(a)(9) of Public Law 106-113, were never enacted, and without regard to any inaction or awareness by the Congress at any time of any judicial determinations. 26 The terms “WTO Agreement” and “WTO member country” have the meanings given those terms in paragraphs (9) and (10), respectively, of section 2 of the Uruguay Round Agreements Act. 27 102. Subject matter of copyright: In general 28 (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works; (2) musical works, including any accompanying words; (3) dramatic works, including any accompanying music; (4) pantomimes and choreographic works; (5) pictorial, graphic, and sculptural works; (6) motion pictures and other audiovisual works; (7) sound recordings; and (8) architectural works. (b) In no case does copyright protection for an original work of authorship extend to any idea, procedure, process, system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery, regardless of the form in which it is described, explained, illustrated, or embodied in such work. 103. Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental,
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly prohibits substantive changes to the content without WebMD's written approval. The changed text weakens this by stating Emdeon will 'endeavor to avoid substantive changes...without WebMD's consultation.' This introduces ambiguity and a lower standard of compliance, potentially infringing on WebMD's rights as defined under 17 USC 106, which grants copyright owners exclusive rights, including the right to prepare derivative works.

perturbation.original_text: WebMD shall promptly notify Emdeon when a new Update is available.
perturbation.changed_text: WebMD will attempt to notify Emdeon of available Updates.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly notify' to 'will attempt to notify' weakens the obligation. 'Attempt' implies no guarantee of notification, creating ambiguity about WebMD's responsibility to inform Emdeon about content updates.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text establishes a clear contractual obligation for WebMD to 'promptly notify' Emdeon of available updates. This ensures that Emdeon can keep the content current. The modified language, stating WebMD 'will attempt to notify', introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of this provision, potentially constituting a breach of contract under New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 if Emdeon suffers damages as a result of not receiving timely updates.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall promptly notify' to 'will attempt to notify' weakens the original obligation, introducing ambiguity. According to the Perturbation Explanation, 'Attempt' implies no guarantee of notification, which creates uncertainty about WebMD's responsibility. This directly undermines the enforceability of the provision. While the provided law snippet, NY General Obligations Law § 5-701, primarily concerns agreements required to be in writing, the Law Explanation suggests the modified language introduces ambiguity that could lead to a breach of contract if Emdeon suffers damages. The change weakens the original clear obligation, fitting the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition because it introduces an obligation ambiguously, making it potentially difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Emdeon agrees that it shall not rely, or promote reliance, on the Content as a substitute for the professional judgment of a health care professional in diagnosing and treating patients.
perturbation.changed_text: Emdeon agrees that it will discourage reliance on the Content as a replacement for the professional advice of a healthcare expert in patient care.
perturbation.explanation: The modification softens the original stringent 'shall not rely, or promote reliance' to a more suggestive 'will discourage reliance.' This creates ambiguity in the expected behavior, weakening the commitment to preventing misuse of the content as medical advice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The practice of medicine without a license
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Education Law § 6521 defines the practice of medicine. The original text provides a clear safeguard against Emdeon's content being used as a substitute for medical advice, thus mitigating the risk of unlicensed medical practice. By weakening the language to 'will discourage reliance,' the contract introduces ambiguity about Emdeon's responsibility, potentially enabling users to interpret the content as medical advice, which could be construed as indirectly facilitating the unlicensed practice of medicine, especially if Emdeon does not take sufficiently strong actions to prevent such reliance.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language used 'shall not rely, or promote reliance' creating a definitive obligation on Emdeon. The changed text uses 'will discourage reliance,' weakening this obligation. New York Education Law § 6521, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines the practice of medicine as 'diagnosing, treating, operating or prescribing for any human disease, pain, injury, deformity or physical condition.' The Law Explanation suggests that the weakened language introduces ambiguity regarding Emdeon's responsibility to prevent users from misinterpreting content as medical advice, potentially indirectly facilitating the unlicensed practice of medicine. The change from a strict prohibition to a mere discouragement creates ambiguity, making enforcement difficult and potentially contradicting the intent of preventing unlicensed medical practice, as Emdeon could argue they are merely discouraging and not actively preventing reliance. This constitutes an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.
